title-group.article-title: Efficacy of heat-treated Lacticaseibacillus paracasei PS23 for individuals with long coronavirus disease-19 syndrome: a double-blinded randomized control pilot study

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/00t89kj24grid.452449.a0000 0004 1762 5613Department of Medicine, MacKay Medical University, New Taipei, Taiwan
2https://ror.org/015b6az38grid.413593.90000 0004 0573 007XSection of Psychiatry and Suicide Prevention Center, MacKay Memorial Hospital, Taipei, Taiwan
3https://ror.org/015b6az38grid.413593.90000 0004 0573 007XDepartment of Neurology, Mackay Memorial Hospital, Taipei, Taiwan
4https://ror.org/015b6az38grid.413593.90000 0004 0573 007XDepartment of Family Medicine, Mackay Memorial Hospital, Taipei, Taiwan
5https://ror.org/015b6az38grid.413593.90000 0004 0573 007XDepartment of Medical Research, Mackay Memorial Hospital, Taipei, Taiwan
6https://ror.org/015b6az38grid.413593.90000 0004 0573 007XDepartment of Psychiatry, Mackay Memorial Hospital, New Taipei, Taiwan

license.license-p: Open Access This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International License, which permits any non-commercial use, sharing, distribution and reproduction in any medium or format, as long as you give appropriate credit to the original author(s) and the source, provide a link to the Creative Commons licence, and indicate if you modified the licensed material. You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article’s Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

abstract.p: Long coronavirus disease (long COVID) refers to symptoms that persist beyond the acute phase of SARS-CoV-2 infection. This study applied the World Health Organization (WHO) definition, which identifies post-COVID-19 condition as symptoms lasting at least two months and beginning around three months after confirmed infection. Given limited evidence on probiotic use for long COVID, we conducted a double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial to evaluate the effects of heat-treated Lacticaseibacillus paracasei PS23 (HT-PS23) in individuals with confirmed COVID-19 and self-reported prolonged symptoms. Thirty-nine eligible participants were randomly assigned to receive either the heat-treated Lacticaseibacillus paracasei PS23 (HT-PS23) or placebo for six weeks. Assessments included validated neuropsychological tests (including executive function and working memory using Color Trails 1&2 and Digit Span and Coding), self-reported symptom scales of depression, anxiety, and quality of life, and blood biomarkers related to inflammation, immune response, and stress (e.g. cortisol, IFN-γ zonulin). After the six-week trial, HT-PS23 group showed notable improvements in cortisol level (p = 0.006), a reduction in errors of CTT2 (p = 0.018), and symptom severity related to breathing difficulties (p = 0.016) and appetite loss (p = 0.030) compared to the placebo group. HT-PS23 may be a feasible and well-tolerated intervention for relieving cognitive or physical symptoms associated with long COVID. Further studies with larger samples are warranted.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1038/s41598-025-27036-3.

funding-source.institution: University–Industry Cooperation Fund, Mackay Memorial Hospital, Taipei, Taiwan
Department of Medical Research, Mackay Memorial Hospital

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Coronavirus disease2019 (COVID-19), caused by SARS-CoV-2, has affected hundreds of millions globally1. Most infected individuals present with mild symptoms, such as cough, myalgia, headache, sore throat, or diarrhea, and recover within weeks. Approximately 15% need hospitalization, and 5% may develop more severe manifestations of pneumonia, fever, dyspnea, or respiratory failure2,3.A high percentage of patients with COVID-19, ranging from 10 to 90%, experience prolonged cognitive, psychological, or multi-organ symptoms that last for five weeks to 6 months after the initial phase4–6. These symptoms may include chest tightness, shortness of breath, fatigue, difficulty concentrating, anxiety, or cognitive impairments4,5,7, which impact life function and have been commonly referred to as long COVID or post -acute sequelae of COVID-195,8, poses ongoing challenges to public health and patient quality of life.

Multiple mechanisms have been proposed to explain long COVID, including persistent viral presence4,9, chronic inflammation, neuroinflammation4,10, gut microbiota imbalance11,12, and psychological stress13. Among these, gut dysbiosis has gained increased attention due to its role in immune regulation, inflammation, and neurological function14,15, probiotics16, or antidepressants17. Studies have shown that individuals with long COVID symptoms- such as fatigue and cognitive impairment- exhibit altered gut microbiota profiles compared to those who recover fully18. Reduced levels of beneficial bacteria and elevated inflammatory markers have been correlated with disease severity and prolonged recovery19,20. A prospective cohort study by Liu et al. comparing patients without post-acute COVID-19 syndrome showed that those with fatigue or memory loss had different compositions in their gut microbiota. Yeoh et al.21 showed that a variety of bacterial species associated with immune regulation remained low in stool samples from patients confirmed with SARS-CoV-2 infection compared to patients without COVID, even 30 days after recovery from COVID. They also reported that this depletion correlated with disease severity and higher plasma concentrations of inflammatory markers.

Probiotics have demonstrated potential in modulating gut microbiota and reducing inflammation during acute COVID-19, with reported benefits including milder symptoms22, fewer complications23, and lower mortality rates24. However, research on their role in post-COVID recovery remains limited. One randomized controlled trial found that a combination of systemic enzymes and probiotics alleviated fatigue in long COVID patients16, suggesting a possible therapeutic role. Notably, recent evidence also supports the use of heat-killed probiotics in modulating inflammation and mood-related symptoms. For example, Cho et al. (2023) demonstrated that heat-killed probiotics, in combination with n-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, exerted synergistic antidepressant-like effects in rats exposed to chronic mild stress, through modulation of the brain-gut axis, inflammation, and gut microbiome position25. These findings further support the relevance of psychobiotics in addressing post-COVID symptoms such as anxiety and depression.

Among emerging probiotic candidates, heat-treated Lacticaseibacillus paracasei PS23 (HT-PS23) can enhance cognitive functions in aging mice26 and reduce anxiety27 in nurses with high stress28. These findings indicate that HT-PS23 may improve overall health, both physically and psychologically. However, no studies to date have investigated whether psychobiotics, such as L. paracasei, have any effect in managing prolonged symptoms of long COVID syndrome. Given the overlap between long COVID symptoms and the known effects of HT-PS23, this pilot double blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial was designed to evaluate its efficacy in alleviating prolonged symptoms after the COVID infection. The study also aims to explore underlying biochemical mechanisms related to inflammation and oxidative stress.

sec.title: Study design and participants
sec.p: This double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled pilot trial was conducted at National Taiwan Sports University. Participants aged 20 ~ 65 were recruited via public advertisements. Inclusion criteria required a confirmed COVID-19 diagnosis (PCR or antigen test) at least four weeks prior and persistent symptoms lasting at least two months post-infection, consistent with WHO’s definition of long COVID. Exclusion criteria included recent antibiotic or probiotic use, immunodeficiency, allergy to lactic acid bacteria, dementia, psychiatric medication use, or any condition deemed unsuitable by the Principal Investigator. Participants who reported any acute infectious disease (Eg. influenza, gastroenteritis) during the intervention period were also excluded. No such cases were identified during the study. Participants could withdraw voluntarily due to adverse reactions or personal reasons.

sec.title: Randomization and intervention
sec.p: Eligible participants were randomly assigned to receive either heat-treated Lacticaseibacillus paracasei PS23 (HT-PS23) or placebo capsules for six weeks. Randomization was performed using an online generator by an independent researcher. Each HT-PS23 capsule contained 10 billion heat-treated cells; participants took two capsules daily. The selected dosage was based on a previous human clinical trial in which a daily intake of 20 billion HT-PS23 cells demonstrated significant improvements in stress-related symptoms and cortisol levels among nurses under high occupational stress28. Both HT-PS23 and placebo capsules were identical in appearance and contained the same excipients to ensure blinding. The HT-PS23 capsules consisted of 62.5% HT-PS23 powder, 35% microcrystalline cellulose, 1.25% magnesium stearate, and 1.25% silicon dioxide. The placebo capsules contained 97.5% microcrystalline cellulose, 1.25% magnesium stearate, and 1.25% silicon dioxide. All products were provided by Bened Biomedical Ltd. and stored at 4 °C. Adverse events were monitored throughout the study, with no intervention-related events reported.

sec.title: Assessments
sec.p: At baseline (V0) and post-intervention (V1), participants underwent blood sampling and evaluations on neuropsychological functions, physical activities, and gastrointestinal symptoms. Compliance was tracked by capsule return and adverse event reporting.

sec.title: Biomarker analysis
sec.p: Blood samples were collected at consistent times to control for circadian variation. Biomarkers analyzed included (Supplementary Table S1):

list-item.p: Inflammation and immune markers: IFN-γ, IFN-α2, MCP-1, LBP, hs-CRP, D-dimer, cortisol.
Gut permeability and viral response: Zonulin, SARS-CoV-2-RBD-IgG.

sec.p: Serum samples were analyzed to evaluate liver and renal functions, including measurements of glutamic oxaloacetic transaminase (GOT), glutamic pyruvic transaminase (GPT), blood urea nitrogen (BUN), and creatinine (CRE). All assays followed manufacturer protocols.

sec.title: Ethics
sec.p: The study was approved by the Institutional Review Board of Mackay Memorial Hospital (IRB no. 22CT054be) and registered at ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT05813899), in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki.

sec.title: Objective neuropsychological assessments
sec.p: Validated Chinese versions of standardized neuropsychological tests were used to assess cognitive function at baseline (V0) and post-intervention (V1) to detect changes in cognitive performance:

list-item.p: Executive function was assessed using the Color Trails Test (CTT 1 & 2)29, which measures processing speed and cognitive flexibility. Good reliability and validity have been shown in the Chinese-translated version30. We used the CTT1 to evaluate processing speed, and CTT2 to examine changes in cognitive flexibility and executive functioning31,32 before and after the trial.

Working memory and processing speed were evaluated using the Digit Span and Coding subtests from the Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale-IV (WAIS-IV). The Chinese version of WAIS-IV has good reliability and validity33,34. Herein, we assessed participants’ responses to Digit Span and Coding from the WAIS-IV to compare their working memory and processing speed before and after the interventions32.
Global clinical status: The Clinical Global Impression Scale (CGI) is a widely used clinician-rated measure of severity (CGI-S) and improvement (CGI-I)35.

sec.title: Subjective assessments
sec.p: Participants completed a battery of validated self-report questionnaires in Chinese to assess psychological symptoms, physical activity, and quality of life at both time points (V0 and V1):

list-item.p: COVID-related symptoms: A checklist based on CDC36, NHS, WHO, and Taiwan Ministry of Health guidelines37 was composed to rate the severity and impact of acute and prolonged symptoms from 1 to 10.

Physical activity: International Physical Activity Questionnaire (IPAQ) – Taiwan Long Version. The IPAQ is a self-report questionnaire that measures the time spent on mild, moderate, or vigorous physical activity during the past week on different aspects of life, such as work, leisure, household tasks, or transportation38,39.
Sleep quality: Insomnia Severity Index (ISI). Good psychometric validity has been shown in the Chinese-translated version40,41.

Anxiety: State-Trait Anxiety Inventory (STAI). Good validities have been shown to diagnose anxiety disorders, distinguish anxiety from depression, to evaluate the effects of stress and coping interventions, and multiple language versions (including Chinese) have been applied in clinical and research settings28,42,43.

Depression: The Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) has been translated and validated in the Chinese population, showing good reliability, validity, and sensitivity among clinical44 and the general populations45.
Quality of life: The Chinese version of the Quality of Life Enjoyment and Satisfaction Questionnaire – Short Form (QLESQ-SF) has been used to evaluate the impact of treatment46,47.

Gastrointestinal symptoms: Visual Analogue Scale for GI discomfort (VAS-GI). A Chinese version of 10-point Visual Analogue Scale has been used to identify and compare if there were gastrointestinal discomforts and their changes over time28,48.
Patient Global Impression of Change (PGI-C): This is a patient-reported measure that evaluates the change in clinical status after an intervention49.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: At an a priori determined α level of 0.05 and a power of 0.80, a total sample size of 24 is required28,50. For data not following a normal distribution, the non-parametric analysis of Mann-Whitney U test was applied. The baseline characteristics and subjective or objective neuropsychological measures and changes over time between V0 and V1 and between the HT-PS23 and the placebo groups were compared using the Pearson Chi-Squared and Mann-Whitney U tests for categorical and continuous variables, respectively.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: The study process is illustrated in Fig. 1. Thirty-nine participants met the inclusion criteria and signed the informed consent forms. Randomization was performed to separate the participants into placebo and HT-PS23 groups. One participant in the placebo group and three in the HT-PS23 group dropped out after being infected with COVID again during the intervention. Two participants in the HT-PS23 group dropped out due to loss of follow-up for personal reasons, and one person dropped out due to taking antibiotics during the intervention period. One participant in the placebo group was not included in the analysis because of low compliance noted (taken < 80% upon trial completion).

caption.p: Study flow chart. (a) Lost to follow-up due to infected COVID during intervention period: n = 1. (b). Lost to follow-up due to personal reason: n = 2, Taking antibiotics during intervention period: n = 1, Lost to follow-up due to infected COVID during intervention period: n = 3. (c): With low compliance (<80%): n=1.

sec.title: Baseline characteristics
sec.p: Table 1 reveals that the baseline characteristics were not significantly different between the two groups after randomization, except that the HT-PS23 group had significantly more people with more than one COVID infection (p = 0.018). Both groups mainly comprised single young (mean age: 22.6, SD: 2.5) males with college degrees, and almost all received three vaccination doses. Sore throat, fatigue, cough, and phlegm were the four most common symptoms, and none of the symptoms during acute COVID-19 infection were significantly different between the groups. No patients were admitted for hospitalization during the COVID episode. The results of the baseline neuropsychological measures (including depression, anxiety, activities, quality of life, and cognitive functions) were also similar in both groups. Although not significant, the HT-PS23 group spent a longer time performing CTT1 and CTT2 at baseline (Table 1).

caption.p: Comparisons of baseline characteristics between the two groups in those completed the 6-week trial.

tr.td: International Physical Activity Questionnaire (IPAQ) MET total
Symptoms of COVID during acute episode
Visual Analog Scale for Gastrointestinal discomfort
Quality of Life, Enjoyment, and Satisfaction Questionnaire (Q-LES-Q)

table-wrap-foot.p: MET = metabolic equivalent (minutes per week), which is calculated by 3.3*Walking minutes*Walking days + 4.0*Moderate-intensity activities minutes*Moderate-intensity activities days + 8.0*Vigorous-intensity activities minutes*Vigorous-intensity activities days.
Continuous variables were analyzed by Mann-Whitney U tests, Categorial variables were analyzed by Pearson Chi-Squared tests.

sec.title: Changes over time between the two groups
sec.p: Table 2 summarizes changes in symptoms and neuropsychological outcomes from baseline (V0) to post intervention (V1) between the HT-PS23 and placebo groups. In terms of cognitive function, no significant differences were observed between groups in the overall completion times for the Color Trails Test (CTT1 and CTT2). However, the HT-PS23 group demonstrated a significant reduction in color errors on the CTT2, suggesting an improvement in set-shifting ability and executive function (Mann-Whitney U = 78.0, p = 0.018). Other cognitive measures, including the WAIS-IV Digit Span and Coding subtests, as well as clinician-rated global severity (CGI-S), did not show significant changes between the HT-PS23 and placebo groups.

caption.p: Changes of outcomes from pre- to post- tests between the two groups.

table-wrap-foot.p: *p < 0.05; MET = metabolic equivalent (minutes per week); HEPA = Health enhancing physical activity.
Continuous variables were analyzed by Mann-Whitney U tests, Categorial variables were analyzed by Pearson Chi-Squared tests.

sec.p: Regarding physical activity, both groups showed increased engagement in health-enhancing physical activity (HEPA) over the course of the trial. The proportion of participants classified as HEPA-active post-intervention was significantly lower in the HT-PS23 group compared to the placebo group (p = 0.034).

Symptom severity analysis revealed that most prolonged symptoms decreased in the HT-PS23 group. Notably, reductions in breathing difficulty (Mann-Whitney U = 77.0, p = 0.016) and loss of appetite (Mann-Whitney U = 84.0, p = 0.030) were significantly greater in the HT-PS23 group than in the placebo group.
No significant between-group differences were found in psychological and quality of life measures, including insomnia, anxiety, depression, gastrointestinal discomfort, and overall life satisfaction.

Comparisons of mean changes in the biochemical parameters and blood markers before and after the trial are shown in Figs. 2; Table 3. The HT-PS23 group exhibited significant improvements in cortisol levels during the 6-week intervention period (U = 51, p = 0.006). No significant differences in other blood markers were observed between the two groups before and after the trial (Fig. 2; Table 3).

caption.p: Comparisons of mean changes in blood markers between the probiotics and the placebo from baseline to endpoint.
Statistical value details of biomarker outcomes.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled pilot trial is the first to report improvements in cortisol levels, specific physical symptoms, and executive function of long COVID following a 6-week intervention with heat-treated Lacticaseibacillus paracasei PS23 (HT-PS23). Despite baseline differences in COVID history and cognitive performance, the HT-PS23 group showed significant reductions in cortisol, greater improvements in breathing difficulty and appetite, as well as fewer errors on the CTT2, compared to placebo. These findings suggest that HT-PS23 may have unique benefits in relieving long-lasting cognitive or physical symptoms possibly related to COVID infections.

In this study, HT-PS23 administration for six weeks resulted in a significant decrease in the blood cortisol level among participants with long COVID. This finding aligns with previous research showing that HT-PS23 reduced cortisol in individuals under psychological stress28. Cortisol is a key biomarker of stress and hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis activity, which may be dysregulated in long COVID due to chronic inflammation or psychological burden51. The observed decrease suggests that HT-PS23 may help modulate stress-related physiological responses, potentially through microbiota–gut–brain axis interactions26. Recent evidence further supports the role of heat-killed probiotics, when combined with n-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, led to notable improvements in behavioral and biological markers of depression in rodent model of chronic mild stress. These effects were attributed to changes in gut microbiota composition and reductions in systemic inflammation, underscoring the influence of the gut-brain axis25. Their findings support the broader application of probiotics like HT-PS23 to influence neuroimmune pathways and psychological outcomes, particularly in conditions characterized by chronic stress and inflammation such as long COVID. However, further studies are needed to confirm these effects and clarify the underlying mechanisms.

Greater improvement in self-reported breathing difficulty was noted in the HT-PS23 group than in the placebo group. Although direct evidence linking probiotics to respiratory symptoms in long COVID is limited, literature suggests that probiotics might help influence respiratory health through inhibiting thrombotic destructions on lung permeability at the recovery or repair stage after COVID52,53. Some theories have suggested that interactions between the lung immune system and gut microbiome may affect respiratory conditions54. For example, probiotic intake has been associated with a 2-fold decreased risk of respiratory tract infections55 and reduced the duration of symptoms, including dyspnea or ear pain. The observed benefit in breathing difficulty may reflect HT-PS23’s potential to modulate immune responses56,57 through microbial competition58,59 or alter the functions of the respiratory tract60–62. It has also been suggested that interactions between probiotics and formations of granulocyte-microphages might strengthen the epithelial barrier to defend against respiratory infections. Gohil et al.63 hypothesized that by expressing ACE2, L. paracasei entering the host cell may become a receptor for the spike protein of SARS-CoV-2 to reduce the influence viral infection in humans. These mechanisms might also explain the effects of probiotics on prolonged respiratory symptoms after COVID.

Improvements in loss of appetite were significantly greater in the HT-PS23 group compared to the placebo group. Loss of appetite is among the most commonly reported nutrition-related complaints during and after COVID hospitalization64,65, yet few studies have explored targeted interventions for this symptom65. Previous research has suggested that probiotics may influence appetite regulation, including a randomized trial showing benefits in patients with major depressive disorder66. A case report described that a combination of high-fiber formula, Chinese herbal foods, and prebiotics helped alleviate symptoms of decreased appetite, palpitations, and anxiety by modifying the gut flora and microbial fermentation activities in a patient with severe long-lasting post-COVID syndrome67. Notably, HT-PS23 has been shown to increase ghrelin levels- a key appetite-regulating hormone- in animal models68,69, which may partially explain the observed effects, although ghrelin was not measured in this study. The dysbiotic gut microbiota has been linked to long COVID outcomes12,21,67,70. Probiotics may help restore microbial balance, enhance intestinal barrier integrity, and regulate metabolic responses65,71,72. Given the potential consequences of prolonged appetite loss, including malnutrition and sarcopenia, probiotic supplementation may offer a supportive strategy for managing gastrointestinal symptoms in long COVID, though further research is warranted.

Analysis of cognitive performance showed that participants in the HT-PS23 group made significantly fewer color errors on the CTT2, indicating improved executive function compared to the placebo group. This finding indicates that L. paracasei might improve prolonged cognitive dysfunction related to executive function. Such finding may be in line with previous animal or human studies showing that probiotics can increase brain-derived neurotropic factors (BDNF) and may help prevent cognitive decline73 or memory impairments26,74–76. Animal studies have shown that L. paracasei improves spatial learning and memory, potentially through elevated levels of dopamine, serotonin, and BDNF in the brain26,77. These mechanisms may help explain the cognitive benefits observed in individuals with long COVID. Although the average improvement in CTT2 color errors was modest, the result suggests that HT-PS23 might contribute to maintaining or enhancing executive function. While no statistically significant changes were observed in blood biomarkers related to inflammation and oxidative stress, the improvements in symptom severity and cognitive function suggest that HT-PS23 may exert its effects through alternative pathways. One possibility is the modulation of the HPA axis, which plays a central role in stress regulation and has been implicated in long COVID-related fatigue and cognitive symptoms. Additionally, psychobiotics may influence neuroendocrine signaling and neurotransmitter synthesis via microbial metabolites such as short-chain fatty acids, which can act locally or systemically to affect brain functions. These mechanisms may operate independently of measurable changes in peripheral inflammatory markers, and warrant further investigation in future studies.

The absence of significant differences in GI symptoms between groups or across time points supports the feasibility and tolerability of HT-PS23 supplementation, consistent with previous findings28. The IPAQ results showed a lower proportion of participants in the HT-PS23 group engaging in health-enhancing physical activity post-intervention, which may reflect lifestyle factors or individual preferences rather than treatment effects. It is also possible that improved cognitive function in this group facilitated engagement in more sedentary, cognitively demanding tasks. In addition, no significant changes were observed in mood, sleep, or quality of life measures, contrasting with previous studies suggesting potential benefits of probiotics or nutritional supplements for fatigue and neurological symptoms in long COVID14,16. This may be due to the relatively mild symptom burden in our sample, or the short duration of intervention. While probiotics may exert anti-inflammatory or antioxidant effects, these mechanisms may not translate into immediate improvements in subjective well-being. External factors such as stress, life events, or post-pandemic adjustments could also influence mood-related outcomes. Further research is needed to clarify the role of HT-PS23 in managing broader aspects of long COVID.

sec.title: Strengths and limitations
sec.p: This prospective, double-blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled pilot trial was designed to investigate the possible effects of L. paracasei on long COVID symptoms, and to extensively evaluate physical or neuropsychological functions before and after the 6-week probiotic versus the placebo. Participants in this study were a homogenous group of people in their mid-20s, whose COVID infection was not severe enough to require hospitalization. However, the small sample size was a major limitation, and a larger sample size is required to confirm the efficacy of probiotics on prolonged symptoms after COVID. Nevertheless, the current pilot study can still be viewed as an exploration of probiotic acceptability and tolerability among those possibly suffering from long COVID syndrome. Second, while we demonstrated significant differences in changes over time in physical or cognitive functions among young college students, further generalizations on the possible effects of HT-PS23 on long COVID symptoms in different age ranges or other physical conditions may still be needed due to study confinement. Third, although we demonstrated improvements in CTT2 before and after the trial, we could not determine whether they could help those with serious cognitive impairments (z-scores < −1.5, standard deviation compared to the norm). This may be because our participants were primarily young people without significant cognitive impairment at baseline. Fourth, symptoms of long COVID indeed encompass a diverse array of symptoms, reflecting the complex pathophysiology of the condition. The heterogeneity in how long COVID may be coded clinically complicates the standardization of the outcome measures. Most of our participants did not have records on their clinical symptoms. Reasons may be that they perceived their symptoms as mild or manageable and did not seek medical attention; there could be barriers to access healthcare; or there may be lack of awareness and recognitions. We used the main outcomes that were surrogate markers and may not be able to proof their clinical significance. However, these surrogate markers, like difficulty breathing and poor appetite, are directly related to the patient’s daily experience and could have a significant impact on their quality of life. Whenever possible, complement surrogate markers with hard endpoints, such as hospitalization rates or recovery times, to strengthen the argument for clinical significance may be needed in future studies. Finally, we employed a wide range of assessments, including blood biological markers that may be linked to physical or mental health, daily activities, inflammation, or metabolism; however, other uncertain mechanisms and confounders remain, including individual dietary habits or stressors. Future evaluations, such as dietary records or assessment of clinical implications based on changes in microorganism distribution, may reveal the possible causal relationships.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: This pilot randomized controlled trial provides preliminary evidence supporting the feasibility and safety of using the psychobiotic Lacticaseibacillus paracasei PS23 (HT-PS23) to manage selected symptoms associated with long COVID. Participants receiving HT-PS23 showed improvements in cortisol levels, executive function, breathing difficulty, and appetite compared to placebo. While these findings are promising, they should be interpreted with caution due to the small sample size and short intervention period. Further research with larger, more diverse populations and extended follow-up is needed to confirm these effects and explore the underlying biological mechanisms, including inflammation, immunomodulation, and microbiota interactions.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: Below is the link to the electronic supplementary material.

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: The authors thank Bened Biomedical Co. Ltd. provided the investigational capsules without compensation.

notes.title: Author contributions
notes.p: SIW designed the study. SIW and WLC conducted the experiments. SIW analyzed the data. SIW interpreted the results and drafted the manuscript. SIW, CJL, YJL, ICL, LCW, and WLC reviewed and edited the manuscript. All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: This study was funded by the University–Industry Cooperation Fund, Mackay Memorial Hospital, Taipei, Taiwan (IAC-11302). SIW was partly funded by the Department of Medical Research, Mackay Memorial Hospital (MMH-113-19, MMH-MM-11103, MMH-111-51, MMH-MM-11002, MMH-110-110, MMH-109112, MMH-10914). The funders did not play a role in the study design; collection, analyses, or interpretation of data; writing of the manuscript; or decision to publish the results.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: The data that support the findings of this study are available on request from the corresponding author. The data are not publicly available due to privacy or ethical restrictions.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
notes.p: This study has been approved by the Institutional Review Board of Mackay Memorial Hospital (IRB no. 22CT054be). The clinical trial registration number for this study is NCT05813899 (16/01/2023).
